original_hypothesis.title: EIP-712 permit in test GTC uses rawAmount in struct hash while storing safe96(amount), risking signature ambiguity/incompatibility
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: GTC.permit() computes structHash using rawAmount, but writes allowances[owner][spender] = safe96(rawAmount). If rawAmount exceeds 2^96-1, safe96() reverts, but more subtly, using rawAmount instead of the truncated amount can create unexpected signature semantics for integrators and may differ from expectations of EIP-2612-style permits.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is test code, but it is used to model integrations. Ambiguous or nonstandard permit semantics can break downstream assumptions or cause signature verification mismatches in tooling/tests, potentially masking real integration issues for IdentityStaking ecosystems.
deployment_assumptions: The test token is used beyond unit tests (e.g., staging or production-like environments) where signatures are relied upon
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: GTC.sol is labeled test-only; no evidence it is excluded from non-test deployments besides comments.

proof_of_signal.pattern: structHash encodes rawAmount; stored allowance uses uint96 amount = safe96(rawAmount)

preconditions: Relying parties use permit signatures for approvals
Potentially large rawAmount values are used or tooling assumes different encoding

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: mismatched value used for signature hash vs stored allowance representation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: nonstandard signature semantics; integration test false confidence or incompatibility
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit EIP-2612 conformance; no domain separator caching; no explicit rawAmount bounds prior to hashing

analysis_questions: Is this permit intended to be EIP-2612 compatible or only a loose test helper?
Can any signature be replayed or misinterpreted across chains/contracts (domain uses chainId and verifyingContract, so likely not)?
Does the amount encoding mismatch affect signed intent in any edge cases?
validation_requirements: Compare behavior against EIP-2612 reference implementation
Fuzz permit with boundary rawAmount values around 2^96-1
Ensure production systems do not rely on this mock for security assumptions

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: In a staging environment using this token, signatures generated by standard libraries may fail unexpectedly or be misinterpreted, potentially leading to incorrect allowance expectations and masking approval-related security test cases.
prerequisites: Use of mock token in environments where permit matters

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Limited; mostly integration correctness unless misused in production
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Developers/operators; potentially users if deployed incorrectly
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Replace mock with standard ERC20Permit implementation

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely no conformance tests vs EIP-2612 reference vectors

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
